title: Scalable dual-prefix hybrid multi-GPU rerun

queries: candidate aware multiclass dual prefix multigpu rerun oom fallback shared prefix
autocomplete multiclass dual prefix ddp rerun memory blocked single gpu

evidence.why_now: EXP111 was implementation-blocked. EXP114 and EXP117 then showed that even the corrected and memory-safer dual-prefix family remains resource-blocked on item-incoterms under single-GPU screening. The agreed Stage 3 rule is now that justified OOM-blocked screening reruns may escalate to multi-GPU, so the next correct step is a clean multi-GPU rerun of the same architecture rather than another single-GPU retry.

hypothesis: Keeping the same dual-prefix architecture but escalating only the OOM-blocked item-incoterms run to multiple currently idle GPUs should make the bundle runnable without changing its scientific content. This tests whether the family itself is viable once the single-GPU memory bottleneck is removed.
notes: This is a clean multi-GPU rerun of the same dual-prefix architecture after repeated single-GPU OOM/resource-blocked outcomes on item-incoterms.
Keep user-churn and user-ltv on routine single-GPU screening unless the launcher needs to reassign them to other idle GPUs.
Do not mix any new loss, sampling, packaging, or pretrain changes into this rerun.
Launched on 2026-05-08 through the local Stage 3 launcher.
Resolved idle-GPU placement at launch time: user-churn -> lab25211 GPU 0, user-ltv -> lab25211 GPU 5, item-incoterms -> lab25211 GPUs 6,7.
First stable orchestrator status check after launch showed all three windows up and item-incoterms running on the intended two-GPU allocation.

Completed on 2026-05-08 as resource-blocked rather than scientifically failed: user-churn and user-ltv finished, but item-incoterms still ran out of memory before its first evaluation even under a 2-GPU DDP launch.

This means the dual-prefix full-candidate raw-label autoregressive recursion is not just single-GPU-tight; it remains practically non-viable in its current form and should not be extended with more near-duplicate reruns.
